title: Low-Pressure Fault Diagnostic
description: Diagnose a low suction-pressure fault or low-pressure cutout (e.g. Carrier T02, Trane A101, Daikin E4, Lennox E200) on a split or packaged system.
safety_notes: Confirm proper PPE (refrigerant gloves, eye protection) before opening any panel.
Apply LOTO to all electrical sources before touching wiring or electrical components.
If the refrigerant circuit must be opened, recover refrigerant first per EPA 608.

steps.title: Verify the unit is calling for cooling
steps.instruction: Look at the thermostat or controller. Tell me the setpoint and the current space temperature, and whether the unit is actually calling for cooling.
steps.expected_input: setpoint, current temperature, and whether cooling is being called

branches.condition: Unit IS calling for cooling (setpoint is below current space temp)
Unit is NOT calling for cooling (satisfied or fan-only)

steps.title: No active cooling demand
steps.instruction: The unit isn't calling for cooling, so a low suction reading is expected. Lower the setpoint below the space temperature, let it run 10 minutes to stabilize, then re-run this diagnostic.

steps.title: Measure suction-side pressure
steps.instruction: With the unit running and stabilized for 10+ minutes, connect the manifold low-side hose to the suction service port. Read the suction pressure in PSIG and confirm the refrigerant type from the nameplate.
steps.expected_input: suction pressure in PSIG and the refrigerant type
steps.safety_note: Confirm PPE before connecting to the service port.

calc_ref.hint: Convert the suction PSIG to a saturation temperature for the stated refrigerant to judge whether it is genuinely low.

branches.condition: Suction pressure is clearly below the normal range for the refrigerant and ambient
Suction pressure is within the normal range (the low-pressure fault may be intermittent or sensor-related)

steps.title: Check evaporator airflow
steps.instruction: Low suction pressure is very often an airflow problem. Check the air filter, evaporator coil cleanliness, and blower operation. Is airflow across the evaporator restricted?
steps.expected_input: state of filter, coil, and blower

branches.condition: Airflow IS restricted (dirty filter/coil, blower not running correctly)
Airflow is adequate (clean filter and coil, blower running normally)

steps.title: Restore evaporator airflow
steps.instruction: Restore airflow: replace the filter, clean the evaporator coil, and confirm the blower runs at the correct speed. Re-stabilize for 10 minutes and recheck suction pressure. If it returns to normal, the low-pressure fault is resolved.

steps.title: Measure superheat
steps.instruction: Clamp a calibrated thermometer on the suction line 6 inches from the compressor. Read the suction line temperature, then use the suction pressure to get superheat. Tell me the superheat value.

calc_ref.hint: superheat = suction line temp − saturation temp at suction pressure.

branches.condition: Superheat is in the normal range (roughly 8–15°F)

steps.title: Probable undercharge / refrigerant loss
steps.instruction: Low suction pressure with high superheat points to undercharge or restricted suction. The most likely cause is a refrigerant leak. Recommend running the leak-check procedure before adding refrigerant. Do NOT just top off the charge without finding the leak.

steps.title: Check the low-pressure switch / sensor
steps.instruction: Pressure reads normal but a low-pressure fault was logged, so suspect the low-pressure switch, transducer, or its wiring. Apply LOTO, then inspect the switch and wiring and check the transducer reading against your gauge. Is the sensor or wiring faulty?
steps.expected_input: condition of the LP switch/transducer and wiring
steps.safety_note: Apply LOTO before inspecting any electrical component.

branches.condition: The LP switch/transducer or its wiring is faulty
Sensor and wiring check out fine

steps.title: Replace the faulty LP sensor / repair wiring
steps.instruction: Replace the faulty low-pressure switch/transducer or repair the wiring, then clear the fault and verify normal operation. The low-pressure fault is resolved.

steps.title: Inconclusive — escalate
steps.instruction: Charge indicators and airflow look normal but the low-pressure fault persists. This is beyond a first-line diagnostic. Recommend escalating to a senior technician or the manufacturer's tech support, and capture your gauge readings before ending the session.
